content.problemStatement:
For an array AAA of length NNN, we define f(A)f(A)f(A) to be the smallest non-negative integer KKK such that AAA can be sorted in ascending order by using the following operation several (possibly, zero) times:


Choose an index iii (1≤i<N1 \le i \lt N1≤i<N) such that Ai⊕Ai+1≤KA_i \oplus A_{i+1} \le KAi​⊕Ai+1​≤K.
Then, swap AiA_iAi​ with Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1​.
Here, ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.

You're given two integers NNN and KKK.

Find any array AAA of length NNN satisfying the following properties:


1≤Ai≤N1 \le A_i \le N1≤Ai​≤N for each 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N, and
Ai≠AjA_i \ne A_jAi​=Aj​ for 1≤i<j≤N1 \le i \lt j \le N1≤i<j≤N, and
f(A)=Kf(A) = Kf(A)=K.
If there are multiple possible solutions, you may find any of them.

If no solution exists, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the length of the array AAA and the required value of f(A)f(A)f(A), respectively.


Output Format
For each test case,



If no solution exists, print the single integer −1-1−1 on a new line.
Otherwise, output NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ - the solution you found.

If there are multiple possible solutions, you may find any of them.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For A=[1,2,3,4]A = [1, 2, 3, 4]A=[1,2,3,4], we don't need any swaps. So, we have f(A)=0=Kf(A) = 0 = Kf(A)=0=K, as needed.
Test case 222: There are two possible arrays with N=2N = 2N=2: [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and [2,1][2, 1][2,1].

For A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2], we have f(A)=0f(A) = 0f(A)=0 since no swaps are needed.
For A=[2,1]A = [2, 1]A=[2,1], we're forced to swap 111 and 222 to sort it. Since 1⊕2=31\oplus 2 = 31⊕2=3, we need to choose some KKK that's ≥3\ge 3≥3. Of course, the smallest valid choice is hence K=3K = 3K=3; so we obtain f(A)=3f(A) = 3f(A)=3.
It can hence be seen that there's no length 222 array satisfying the conditions, so we print −1-1−1.

Test case 333: For A=[3,2,5,1,4]A = [3, 2, 5, 1, 4]A=[3,2,5,1,4], it can be proved that AAA can be sorted in ascending order by choosing K=4K = 4K=4, whereas it cannot be sorted with K=3K = 3K=3.
This makes f(A)=4f(A) = 4f(A)=4, as required.
